metadata.description: Case A: Interpretive Mutation - The constitutional text remained unchanged, but the binding interpretation governing imprisonment after second-instance conviction underwent two mutations (2016 and 2019)
metadata.demonstration: Shows how Statutory Versions (constitutional text) remain active while being overlaid by Interpretive Versions (judicial interpretations) without textual amendment

metadata.description: Constitutional provision on presumption of innocence
Supreme Court ruling allowing imprisonment after second-instance conviction

items.label: Vote of Rapporteur Minister Teori Zavascki

metadata.description: Majority vote establishing the new interpretation
Supreme Court ruling reversing HC 126.292, prohibiting imprisonment before final judgment

items.label: Vote of Rapporteur Minister Marco Aurélio

metadata.description: Majority vote restoring strict interpretation of presumption of innocence

textUnits.content: ninguém será considerado culpado até o trânsito em julgado de sentença penal condenatória
no one shall be considered guilty until the final judgment of a criminal conviction

The provisional execution of a criminal conviction issued at the appellate level (second instance), even when subject to special or extraordinary appeals, does not compromise the constitutional principle of presumption of innocence. After second-instance confirmation, the principle of non-culpability is exhausted, as appeals to higher courts do not address facts and evidence, but only matters of law.
Imprisonment is allowed after confirmation of conviction by second-instance court, before final judgment (trânsito em julgado).

Based on the presumption of innocence principle (Article 5, LVII, of the Federal Constitution), sentence execution can only begin after final judgment (trânsito em julgado) of a criminal conviction. The constitutional text requires exhaustion of all appeal possibilities.
Imprisonment before final judgment (trânsito em julgado) violates the constitutional presumption of innocence.

[Full text of Minister Teori Zavascki's vote explaining that maintaining a criminal sentence at the second instance concludes the analysis of facts and evidence establishing the defendant's guilt, authorizing the beginning of sentence execution]

The provisional execution of a criminal conviction issued at the appellate level, even when subject to special or extraordinary appeals, does not compromise the constitutional principle of presumption of innocence. After second-instance confirmation, the principle of non-culpability is exhausted, as appeals to higher courts (STJ or STF) do not address facts and evidence, but only matters of law.
[Full text of Minister Marco Aurélio's vote explaining that the constitutional text 'no one shall be considered guilty until final judgment' requires exhaustion of all appeal possibilities]

Based on the presumption of innocence principle (Article 5, LVII), which establishes that 'no one shall be considered guilty until final judgment of a criminal conviction,' sentence execution can only begin after final judgment. The principle applies throughout all stages of criminal proceedings.

sampleQueries.interpretation: On June 1, 2017, both the original statutory text (S-TV) and the binding interpretation (I-TV) coexist. The interpretation provides the binding meaning: imprisonment allowed after second-instance conviction.
After November 2019, the statutory text (S-TV) remains active, overlaid by the reversed interpretation (I-TV): imprisonment prohibited before final judgment.

Timeline of actions affecting this Item: Interpretation action by HC 126.292 (2016-02-17) creating interpretive version allowing early imprisonment, followed by Overruling action by ADCs 43/44/54 (2019-11-07) terminating the previous interpretation and creating new one prohibiting early imprisonment. The original statutory version (1988-10-05) was never modified by an action, only overlaid by judicial interpretations.
